IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important, practical problem—overlapping NER—and proposes a novel multigraph structured prediction model that is, to my knowledge from the summary, the first to use multigraphs for this task while maintaining linear-time O(nT) inference. Empirically it shows consistent gains over strong baselines (up to 2.1 F1 on ACE2004) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA), suggesting practitioners and researchers could adopt or build on the approach. The work opens clear follow-up directions (extensions, interactions it currently omits) though lack of stated code release and noted modeling limitations slightly reduce immediate reproducibility and broad adoption, so the impact is strong but not fully transformative. | B(4): Overlapping-entity recognition is an important, persistent problem and introducing a multigraph-based structured prediction model that retains linear-time inference while outperforming strong baselines (up to 2.1 F1 on ACE2004) is a substantive contribution. From a structured-prediction perspective, the use of multigraphs as a modeling primitive is a meaningful extension of existing formalisms (e.g., hypergraphs, constituency-based models) and should reshape how researchers think about representing overlapping spans. The work is likely to stimulate follow-up research exploring multigraph expressiveness and trade-offs, and to be adopted or built upon in the overlapping-NER subfield, though it stops short of a transformative shift across broader NLP modeling paradigms.

The work opens clear follow-up directions (extensions, interactions it currently omits) though lack of stated code release and noted modeling limitations slightly reduce immediate reproducibility and broad adoption, so the impact is strong but not fully transformative.

The work is likely to stimulate follow-up research exploring multigraph expressiveness and trade-offs, and to be adopted or built upon in the overlapping-NER subfield, though it stops short of a transformative shift across broader NLP modeling paradigms.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental evaluation is strong in coverage: three standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, F&M '09, linear CRF) support the main empirical claims, and training/inference complexity and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not indicate ablation studies or sensitivity analyses isolating model components, and code release is not mentioned, which hampers exact reproducibility despite adequate methodological detail. The paper does acknowledge at least one modeling limitation, but more thorough failure-case analysis and released code would be needed for full reproducibility and robustness evaluation. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is solid: three standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004, ACE2005, GENIA) and strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parser, Illinois NER) are used, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses to isolate which modeling choices drive the gains, and code is not reported which weakens reproducibility despite detailed training/inference descriptions. Related work is described as adequate and a limitation of the model is explicitly acknowledged, indicating reasonable thoroughness overall but with minor gaps in analysis and release for full reproducibility.

However, the summary does not indicate ablation studies or sensitivity analyses isolating model components, and code release is not mentioned, which hampers exact reproducibility despite adequate methodological detail. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a canonical NLP task (overlapping NER) with a novel structured prediction formalism (multigraphs) and demonstrates empirical gains on standard benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF, semi-CRF, Finkel & Manning). The methodology (log-linear model, L-BFGS training, inside–outside style inference with O(nT) complexity) and evaluation (P/R/F1, hyperparameters reported) match community standards for a top venue. From a reproducibility standpoint it would be better if code were released, but the paper otherwise provides the datasets, baselines, and complexity analysis needed by the venue’s readership. Overall the topic, audience, formulation, and evaluation are well aligned with top NLP/ML conference scope. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP modeling problem—overlapping named-entity recognition—using a novel structured-prediction formalism (multigraphs) and compares against appropriate strong baselines (mention hypergraph, semi-CRF, constituency parser) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) with standard metrics. The claimed algorithmic contribution (linear-time O(nT) inference, generalized inside-outside) and empirical gains make it directly relevant to top NLP/ML audiences interested in representation and inference trade-offs for structured prediction. The scope, methodology, and evaluation meet the community standards, and the work is well positioned to interest researchers in structured models and overlapping-entity recognition.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a canonical NLP task (overlapping NER) with a novel structured prediction formalism (multigraphs) and demonstrates empirical gains on standard benchmarks (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) against strong baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF, semi-CRF, Finkel & Manning). The methodology (log-linear model, L-BFGS training, inside–outside style inference with O(nT) complexity) and evaluation (P/R/F1, hyperparameters reported) match community standards for a top venue. From a reproducibility standpoint it would be better if code were released, but the paper otherwise provides the datasets, baselines, and complexity analysis needed by the venue’s readership. Overall the topic, audience, formulation, and evaluation are well aligned with top NLP/ML conference scope.

The claimed algorithmic contribution (linear-time O(nT) inference, generalized inside-outside) and empirical gains make it directly relevant to top NLP/ML audiences interested in representation and inference trade-offs for structured prediction. The scope, methodology, and evaluation meet the community standards, and the work is well positioned to interest researchers in structured models and overlapping-entity recognition.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear technical claims (a multigraph structured prediction model with O(nT) inference and empirical gains up to 2.1 F1 on ACE2004) and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF variants, constituency parser) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA). The mathematical setup (log-linear model, generalized inside–outside DP, max‑product decoding) is plausible and internally consistent in the summary, but I cannot verify full derivations here; no obvious theoretical errors are reported. Experimental methodology is mostly sound—reasonable baselines and hyperparameter reporting—but the summary omits code release and statistical significance testing and does not detail multiple seeds or runtime/complexity measurements, which limits reproducibility and robustness claims. Given these minor but important gaps, the paper is technically strong but falls short of flawless reproducibility, so I rate it 4. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF, semi-CRF, constituency parser) showing up to 2.1 F1 gain on ACE2004. The presented algorithmic claim—linear-time inference O(nT) via a generalized inside–outside DP and max-product decoding—is plausible and consistent with the comparison to the mention hypergraph, and no obvious mathematical contradictions are indicated in the summary. Minor concerns remain: the summary does not state formal proofs or detailed derivations (only a high-level description), and code/reproducibility is not mentioned, so some implementation-sensitive assumptions could be unverified. Overall the technical development and experimental methodology appear sound, with only minor gaps that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (a multigraph structured prediction model with O(nT) inference and empirical gains up to 2.1 F1 on ACE2004) and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (mention hypergraph, CRF variants, constituency parser) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA). The mathematical setup (log-linear model, generalized inside–outside DP, max‑product decoding) is plausible and internally consistent in the summary, but I cannot verify full derivations here; no obvious theoretical errors are reported. Experimental methodology is mostly sound—reasonable baselines and hyperparameter reporting—but the summary omits code release and statistical significance testing and does not detail multiple seeds or runtime/complexity measurements, which limits reproducibility and robustness claims. Given these minor but important gaps, the paper is technically strong but falls short of flawless reproducibility, so I rate it 4.

Minor concerns remain: the summary does not state formal proofs or detailed derivations (only a high-level description), and code/reproducibility is not mentioned, so some implementation-sensitive assumptions could be unverified. Overall the technical development and experimental methodology appear sound, with only minor gaps that do not undermine the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against strong, relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, Finkel & Manning ’09, Illinois NER) on standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using consistent metrics (P/R/F1) and reports hyperparameters—supporting broadly fair comparisons. The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods are comparable and note a limitation where their model misses some interactions that a linear-chain CRF captures. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the summary does not indicate comparison to more recent neural sequence/tagging approaches (potential omission), so I mark this as a minor but notable gap rather than a major flaw. | B(5): From the summary the paper compares to the key and relevant baselines for overlapping-entity recognition (mention hypergraph, Finkel & Manning 2009 constituency parser, linear-chain CRF variants, semi-CRF, Illinois NER) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) with the same metrics and reported hyperparameters, so comparisons appear fair. The authors acknowledge limitations where their model underperforms certain interaction modeling of linear-chain CRFs and explicitly position inference complexity (O(nT)) relative to prior work, and related work coverage is described as adequate. Overall the empirical and methodological comparisons are comprehensive and well-justified given the stated claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against strong, relevant baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, semi-CRF, Finkel & Manning ’09, Illinois NER) on standard overlapping-entity datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) using consistent metrics (P/R/F1) and reports hyperparameters—supporting broadly fair comparisons. The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods are comparable and note a limitation where their model misses some interactions that a linear-chain CRF captures. While related work coverage is described as adequate, the summary does not indicate comparison to more recent neural sequence/tagging approaches (potential omission), so I mark this as a minor but notable gap rather than a major flaw.

reviewer_b.justification: From the summary the paper compares to the key and relevant baselines for overlapping-entity recognition (mention hypergraph, Finkel & Manning 2009 constituency parser, linear-chain CRF variants, semi-CRF, Illinois NER) on standard datasets (ACE2004/2005, GENIA) with the same metrics and reported hyperparameters, so comparisons appear fair. The authors acknowledge limitations where their model underperforms certain interaction modeling of linear-chain CRFs and explicitly position inference complexity (O(nT)) relative to prior work, and related work coverage is described as adequate. Overall the empirical and methodological comparisons are comprehensive and well-justified given the stated claims.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from introduction to method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (multigraph model, linear-time inference, empirical gains) are stated early. Notation and methodology are described in sufficient detail for reproduction (log-linear model, training with L-BFGS, inside–outside DP, O(nT) inference) and hyperparameters are reported, while related work coverage is adequate. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective, and the writing quality is generally good; one minor presentation gap is the lack of a code release mention and a brief clarification of the stated limitation comparing interactions to linear-chain CRF could improve clarity. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a multigraph-based structured prediction model with linear-time inference and empirical gains) are stated early and carried through the method and experiments. Notation and algorithmic description appear consistent and the generalized inside–outside DP and decoding are explained; figures/tables (reported results on ACE2004/2005 and GENIA) effectively support claims. Writing quality is good with adequate related-work coverage and hyperparameter reporting, though a few sections (e.g., limitations of type interactions vs. linear-chain CRF) could be expanded for clarity. From a presentation standpoint it reads well but has minor areas that would benefit from slightly more detail.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly new structured-prediction formulation—using multigraphs to represent overlapping entities—and claims to be the first to apply multigraphs for this task, which is a notable departure from prior hypergraph and constituency-parser approaches. The method also introduces a non-trivial inference procedure (generalized inside–outside DP achieving O(nT) time) that differentiates it from existing mention-hypergraph and constituency parsing models. From the perspective of an experienced systems engineer, this reads as a substantive and informative methodological innovation rather than a simple combination of known techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new structured-prediction formalism—using multigraphs to represent overlapping entities—which, per the summary, is (to the authors' knowledge) the first such application. It explicitly differentiates itself from prior hypergraph and constituency-parser approaches and claims the non-trivial technical development of an inside–outside style inference yielding O(nT) time, matching mention hypergraphs while expanding representational options. This is a notable advance over existing methods rather than a mere application of known techniques, though the admitted limitation (not modeling certain cross-type interactions) suggests it is an important step forward rather than a complete paradigm shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new structured-prediction formulation—using multigraphs to represent overlapping entities—and claims to be the first to apply multigraphs for this task, which is a notable departure from prior hypergraph and constituency-parser approaches. The method also introduces a non-trivial inference procedure (generalized inside–outside DP achieving O(nT) time) that differentiates it from existing mention-hypergraph and constituency parsing models. From the perspective of an experienced systems engineer, this reads as a substantive and informative methodological innovation rather than a simple combination of known techniques.

It explicitly differentiates itself from prior hypergraph and constituency-parser approaches and claims the non-trivial technical development of an inside–outside style inference yielding O(nT) time, matching mention hypergraphs while expanding representational options. main_claims: Proposes a new multigraph-based structured prediction model capable of recognizing overlapping entities.

The multigraph model achieves better empirical performance than prior baselines (mention hypergraph, linear-chain CRF, and prior constituency-parser based models) on standard overlapping-entity datasets, with up to 2.1 F1 improvement on ACE2004.

The model attains linear-time inference O(nT) (same as the mention hypergraph model) and is, to the authors' knowledge, the first structured prediction model that utilizes multigraphs to predict overlapping structures.
_summary.methodology: Defines a log-linear multigraph-based model with features on edges, trained by maximizing regularized log-likelihood with L-BFGS; inference uses a generalized inside-outside dynamic programming procedure with O(nT) time and decoding via max-product MPE to extract the highest-scoring subgraph (entities).
limitations: The multigraph model does not model some interactions between distinct entity types that a linear-chain CRF baseline models.

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP structured prediction systems; experience deploying sequence and graph models in research and industry settings. Has implemented and reproduced models from major NLP venues and contributed to open-source evaluation toolkits.
traits: checks for clarity and completeness of experimental setup (datasets, splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, seeds)
values strong baselines, fair comparisons, and careful ablation studies isolating the impact of model components
insists on reproducibility: code release, runtime complexity analysis, and manageable implementation details
examines statistical rigor: proper averaging, significance testing, and robustness to hyperparameter choices
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental validity, reproducibility, and whether empirical claims are convincingly supported by comparisons and ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Structured Prediction Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in structured prediction and representation learning for NLP, with a track record of work on parsing, mention/NER modeling, and graphical models. Actively follows advances in overlapping-entity recognition and formal modeling innovations.
traits: assesses novelty and theoretical contribution relative to prior structured prediction formalisms (e.g., hypergraphs, constituency-based methods)
evaluates expressiveness vs. efficiency trade-offs and whether the proposed formalism meaningfully expands modeling capabilities
considers broader impact: applicability across datasets, limits of the approach, and potential to inspire follow-up work
looks for clear connections to prior work and whether claims of being 'first' are well-justified
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by its conceptual novelty, positioning in the literature, and potential long-term impact on the overlapping-entity recognition research agenda.
